column2: fact
The GeForce RTX 5090 uses the Blackwell architecture with 21,760 CUDA cores, 680 5th-generation Tensor cores and 32 GB of GDDR7 memory on a 512-bit bus providing ~1.79 TB/s bandwidth.
RTX 5090 cards have a base clock around 2.01 GHz and boost up to 2.41 GHz.
Each RTX 5090 draws 575 W and requires 4 × PCIe 8-pin or one 600 W PCIe Gen 5 cable; NVIDIA recommends a 1 kW system PSU.
The card lacks NVLink and does not support SLI; GeForce RTX 50‑series GPUs cannot perform GPU↔GPU peer‑to‑peer communication.
Benchmarks show the RTX 5090 is 27–35 % faster than the RTX 4090 but consumes 575 W and can reach 89–90 °C under heavy load.
RunPod’s Qwen2.5‑Coder‑7B benchmarks reported the RTX 5090 achieving 5,841 tokens/second (batch size 8, 1024 tokens) and outperforming an A100 by 2.6×.
The Local LLM Hardware Guide states that dual RTX 5090 systems can achieve 27 tokens/second on quantized 70 B models, matching H100 performance at a fraction of the cost.
Puget Systems reports that a dual RTX 5090 system with a high‑end CPU can require ~1.5 kW for the CPU and GPUs alone, exceeding the capacity of many 1,600 W power supplies and 120 V circuits.
Dual‑5090 workstations often use 2 800 W power supplies and 200–240 V circuits to provide sufficient power and headroom.
A forum build uses two RTX 5090 OC cards on an ASUS Pro WS WRX80E‑SAGE SE WiFi motherboard with a Threadripper Pro 5975WX CPU, 256 GB ECC DDR4, and a 2 000 W PSU.
Tensor parallelism on two RTX 5090s provides a 1.14–1.29× speed‑up for Qwen3‑8B RAG workloads; throughput increases from 411 TPS on a single GPU to 530 TPS on two GPUs.
Dual RTX 5090s achieve 7,438 tokens/sec for high‑concurrency API workloads, only slightly higher than 6,894 TPS on one GPU.
For 8 k‑context RAG, the single RTX 5090 delivers a 450 ms time‑to‑first‑token (TTFT) whereas the dual‑GPU setup increases TTFT to 620 ms because of synchronization overhead.
The Private LLM Inference study found that NVFP4 quantization provides a 1.6× throughput improvement over BF16 with 41 % energy reduction and minimal quality loss on the RTX 5090.
Setting the power limit to around 400 W per RTX 5090 using nvidia‑smi can significantly reduce power draw and heat without a large performance penalty.
vLLM with tensor parallelism (size=2), continuous batching and pinned memory maximizes token throughput on dual GPUs.
Dual RTX 5090 systems enable longer contexts (32 k–64 k) and allow running larger models (70 B parameters) using tensor parallelism and 4‑bit quantization.
High‑flow server fans or custom liquid/immersion cooling are necessary to dissipate heat from two 575 W GPUs; immersion builds use dielectric fluid, massive radiators and separate pumps.
NVIDIA moderators confirm that GeForce 50‑series cards do not support P2P; NCCL must fall back to host memory for inter‑GPU transfers, limiting scaling efficiency.
The RTX 5090 has a $1,999 MSRP, but street prices often range between $2,500 and $3,800 according to market reports.
